ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mutton Rogan Josh Prata Set. FOODON_03315595 (lamb dish) is the closest parent category covering mutton/lamb-based prepared dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Meat dishes, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Rogan Josh or mutton curry with prata in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish.

serving.description: 1 set (mutton rogan josh curry with 2 pieces of prata)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of mutton rogan josh curry (approximately 300 g) with two pieces of plain prata (approximately 150 g total).

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: Mutton curry (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) and Plain prata / parotta (HPB Singapore Food Composition Database); weighted composite per 100 g of combined serving.
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Mutton Rogan Josh Prata Set as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighted component analysis: mutton curry component from ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry' (per 100 g: energy 165 kcal, protein 14.5 g, fat 10.8 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g, dietary fibre 0.5 g, sodium 420 mg, saturated fat ~4.2 g, sugar ~1.0 g); prata component from HPB Singapore 'Plain prata' (per 100 g: energy 286 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 12.0 g, carbohydrate 38.0 g, dietary fibre 1.5 g, sodium 380 mg, saturated fat ~5.5 g, sugar ~2.0 g). Composite per 100 g calculated at 67% mutton curry weight fraction and 33% prata weight fraction of the 450 g serving (300 g curry : 150 g prata).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous components: plain white bread / flatbread GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database) and meat curry GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate contribution); composite GI weighted by carbohydrate contribution of prata component.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Rogan Josh Prata Set. Composite GI estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted method: prata (plain flatbread) GI ~54 based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain chapati/roti (GI 52–55, mean 54); mutton curry contributes negligible carbohydrate (3.2 g per 100 g curry) and is assigned GI 0 for weighting purposes. Weighted composite GI = (prata carb fraction × prata GI + curry carb fraction × curry GI) / total carb = ((4.95 × 54) + (2.14 × 0)) / 14.6 ≈ 18.3 / 14.6 ≈ 18 — however, the prata flatbread dominates the glycemic response; using the standard analogous-food approach for the prata component alone (GI 54 for plain roti/chapati per University of Sydney) and acknowledging the curry's low carbohydrate dilution effect, a composite GI of 52 is assigned. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 65.7) / 100 = 34.2, where carbohydrate per serving = 14.6 g/100 g × 450 g = 65.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~407 mg per 100 g; ~1832 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from the curry sauce and prata preparation; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to half a serving and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day to stay within the 2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~34 per full serving) driven by refined wheat flour prata
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this set meal as the prata component is made from refined wheat flour and contributes a substantial glycemic load of approximately 34 per serving; consuming only one piece of prata instead of two and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee, mutton fat, and prata oil (~4.6 g per 100 g; ~20.7 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its significant saturated fat content from ghee used in the rogan josh and oil used in prata preparation; occasional consumption in reduced portions is preferable, and requesting preparation with less ghee or oil may help reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Rogan Josh Prata Set is a Singaporean-Indian restaurant meal combining two classic dishes: Mutton Rogan Josh, a slow-braised Kashmiri mutton curry, and prata, a crispy pan-fried South Indian flatbread. Rogan Josh originates from the Kashmir Valley and is characterised by its deep red colour derived from Kashmiri dried red chillies and its aromatic spice profile featuring fennel seeds, ginger powder, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves, slow-cooked with bone-in mutton in ghee and yogurt until the meat is tender and the sauce is rich and fragrant. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a set meal at Indian Muslim and North Indian restaurants, pairing the curry with two pieces of plain prata — a layered, flaky flatbread made from refined wheat flour, egg, and oil, cooked on a flat griddle. The combination delivers a contrast of textures: the tender, fall-off-the-bone mutton in a thick, spiced gravy alongside the crispy, chewy prata ideal for scooping. Nutritionally, the set is energy-dense at approximately 205 kcal per 100 g and provides moderate protein (~11.5 g/100 g) from the mutton, significant fat (~11.1 g/100 g) including saturated fat from ghee and mutton, and moderate carbohydrates (~14.6 g/100 g) primarily from the prata. Sodium content is high (~407 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load per full serving is approximately 34, driven by the refined flour prata, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content is a consideration for cardiovascular health. This dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is a popular choice for lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Mutton Rogan Josh Prata Set as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using ICMR-NIN (mutton curry) and HPB Singapore (plain prata) data, both approved primary sources. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain roti/chapati) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-level variation in preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN mutton curry data and HPB Singapore plain prata data at a 67:33 weight ratio (300 g curry : 150 g prata per 450 g serving). Actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, ghee quantity, and prata size. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in the ICMR-NIN or HPB component sources used. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not separately reported in the component sources. 4. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources; trans fat from commercial prata preparation may be present but is unquantified. 5. potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 6. calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 7. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 8. cholesterol_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 9. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 10. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 11. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 12. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 13. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 14. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 15. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 16. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 17. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 18. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 19. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 20. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 21. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 22. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 23. water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 24. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on plain roti/chapati GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 25. Halal status reflects typical Singapore Indian Muslim restaurant practice; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 26. The cuisine_tag 'Naan' in the input is noted as inaccurate for this dish — the bread component is prata (paratha), not naan; this has been corrected in the output.
